basing a study on % change in VIX is not ok

the vix index is already stated in % terms (annualized volatility). so he’s taking percent on percent

+30% VIX move 10->13 is not the same as VIX 60->78. the indicate totally different things https://t.co/gX6kIIDCZW
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
For people who are worried about the market today, I get it. This stuff is very stressful. 

So I put together a chart of all of the times the VIX (the "fear index" of the market) was up over 30% in a day (like today) in the past ten years. 

23 out of 25 instances the market was https://t.co/6HZrLMP4gP

tweets.text:
i don’t understand why people keep saying that

it’s an illiquid shitcoin with no buyers. obviously if you’re gonna try to buy 5% of the supply in a few short months you’re going to push prices to range highs where you’ll finally find some people willing to sell and fuck off forever, at which point almost all of the volume will be there and you’ll end up getting filled at the top

it kind of has to be that way mathematically

it’s also why it feels like all of the btc and eth OGs disappeared. saylor and tom found the prices where they’re willing to sell and fuck off. and then they did

thread_tweets.text: how else would you accumulate 5%? people aren’t gonna sell it to you at range lows, definitely not in a few short months. you’d have to accumulate for years to achieve that? and that’s impractical because remember he was only able to raise the capital in the first place by being insanely bullish. he’d never raise enough to slowly accumulate a similar amount at the lows even though it’s hypothetically much cheaper. and like, to do that it needs to be a secret, which rules out raising from public markets

this sounds exciting but admittedly understanding this fully is beyond my paygrade

a simple question to help me understand and my apologies if it’s dumb: would this verification have caught the hidden inflation bug that was just discovered, or is it not robust enough to catch that if it’s not previously known?
thread_tweets.full_text:
this sounds exciting but admittedly understanding this fully is beyond my paygrade

a simple question to help me understand and my apologies if it’s dumb: would this verification have caught the hidden inflation bug that was just discovered, or is it not robust enough to catch that if it’s not previously known?

thread_tweets.text:
yeah thank you, i do understand formal verification conceptually but wasn’t sure from the previous reply how much of the actual implementation is being covered in this case if that makes sense

anyway, if this would’ve caught this then that’s very impressive, so we’re expecting that if there are similar bugs in orchard, this verification process will catch them as well, right?

that’s very exciting and sounds very promising. in that case what further improvement does a tachyon pool provide in the context of formal verification, if orchard/ironwood will already be formally verified?

thread_tweets.text: @SteelyDanMxmlst @alysdenby She would know them as Ulster Scots. We call them Scots Irish to distinguish them from the later wave of Catholic Irish migration. And indeed it’s a very specific cultural and demographic group with huge influence on American frontier culture
thread_tweets.full_text: @SteelyDanMxmlst @alysdenby She would know them as Ulster Scots. We call them Scots Irish to distinguish them from the later wave of Catholic Irish migration. And indeed it’s a very specific cultural and demographic group with huge influence on American frontier culture

thread_tweets.text: @Somatichealer0 @LeoKearse @alysdenby Scots Irish is what Americans call Ulster Scots who immigrated to the US in the 1700s (many of them did as they were itinerant to begin with) . It’s used to contrast with the later Irish Catholics who migrated.

tweets.text: In office hours with startups I often try to figure out a secret weapon they can use to protect themselves from competitors. Today to my delight I talked to one whose most natural secret weapon was to be genuinely benevolent.

thread_tweets.text: In office hours with startups I often try to figure out a secret weapon they can use to protect themselves from competitors. Today to my delight I talked to one whose most natural secret weapon was to be genuinely benevolent.
thread_tweets.full_text: In office hours with startups I often try to figure out a secret weapon they can use to protect themselves from competitors. Today to my delight I talked to one whose most natural secret weapon was to be genuinely benevolent.

thread_tweets.text: The founders were already benevolent. The reason they'd started the company was to help other people who had a problem one of the founders had had. But I think they were pleased to learn that it was a strategic advantage for them, and that they should lean into it.
thread_tweets.full_text: The founders were already benevolent. The reason they'd started the company was to help other people who had a problem one of the founders had had. But I think they were pleased to learn that it was a strategic advantage for them, and that they should lean into it.

tweets.text:
I'm already seeing this content all over my TikTok and how Gen Z is going to go absolutely nuts about it.

That's the very definition of a crypto consumer product that will bring millions of people onchain without them even realizing it.

All it took was repackaging something people already love in the real world into a fun onchain experience, and they'll follow.

Who would have thought it could be that simple?

tweets.full_text:
I'm already seeing this content all over my TikTok and how Gen Z is going to go absolutely nuts about it.

That's the very definition of a crypto consumer product that will bring millions of people onchain without them even realizing it.

All it took was repackaging something people already love in the real world into a fun onchain experience, and they'll follow.

Who would have thought it could be that simple?

tweets.text:
The same country that put humans on the Moon in 1969 now takes an average of 4.5 years to approve major infrastructure permits.. longer than it took to build the Panama Canal. 

Transmission lines average 10 years from permitting to completion. The bottleneck to abundance is not technology, but BUREAUCRACY!
